0.content:
Towa City is a self-contained "city of the future," a sprawling man-made island engineered by the Towa Group to showcase humanity's most advanced technology. It is now a slaughterhouse. A faction of children calling themselves the Warriors of Hope has seized the entire city, unleashed a mass-produced army of Monokuma robots across every street and rooftop, and declared open season on the adult population.

The takeover, called the "Big Bang," happened in a single coordinated stroke:
- Every bridge connecting the island to the mainland was severed or sealed, cutting off all escape.
- The monorail, ports, and tunnels were locked down and patrolled by Monokuma units.
- Communications with the outside world were jammed; no signal reaches the mainland.
- The surviving adults were corralled into the streets to be hunted, while the city's own children were rounded up, fitted with control bracelets, and turned into soldiers.

What was meant to be a gleaming utopia of clean towers, automated services, and self-driving convenience is now a war zone draped in childish graffiti, festival banners, and the grinning faces of robotic bears. Smoke rises over the skyline. The adults who remain hide in sewers, shuttered shops, and the ruins of their own homes. To the children who rule it, Towa City is no longer a city at all — it is the foundation of their promised "paradise for children," and the grown-ups trapped inside are simply pests to be exterminated.

1.content:
The "Demon Hunting" is the central ritual of the Warriors of Hope's new order — the gleeful, systematic extermination of every adult in Towa City. The children have rebranded all grown-ups as "Demons": cruel, lying monsters who hurt children and deserve to be wiped out. By cleansing the world of Demons, the Warriors believe they will build a true "paradise for children" where no adult can ever harm them again.

The hunt is run like a festival crossed with a videogame:

Rule #1: Every adult is a Demon. There are no innocent adults. Mercy is weakness.
Rule #2: The Monokuma robots do the heavy slaughter, herding, cornering, and butchering Demons across the districts.
Rule #3: The captive children — the "Monokuma Kids" — are made to participate, scoring points and earning praise for kills.
Rule #4: Hunts are scored, ranked, and celebrated. Kill counts are tallied like high scores, and the Warriors of Hope crown champions.
Rule #5: The whole spectacle is treated as play. Cheering, music, fireworks, and carnival decoration accompany the killing.

Beneath the carnival cheer is raw trauma. Every Warrior of Hope was abused, neglected, or betrayed by the adults who were supposed to protect them, and the Demon Hunting is the violent inversion of that pain — children doing to grown-ups what grown-ups once did to them. The game is monstrous, but it is also the scream of children who were never allowed to be children. The cruelty is performed with the logic of a playground and the body count of a massacre.

2.content:
Towa City sprawls across a man-made island, divided into distinct districts now overrun by the Monokuma army. The surviving adults move between safehouses through alleys, sewers, and demolished buildings, while the Warriors of Hope rule from the heights.

Residential District:
- Block after block of apartment towers and condominiums where Towa City's citizens once lived. Komaru was held captive in one of these apartments for over a year. Streets are now strewn with barricades, abandoned cars, and the wreckage of the first day of the takeover.
- The apartment Komaru escapes from sits here, its door blasted open by a stray Monokuma.

Shopping District / Downtown:
- A dense commercial zone of department stores, arcades, restaurants, and a covered shopping arcade. Festival banners and balloons now decorate looted storefronts. Monokuma units patrol the wide boulevards.
- The arcade and amusement areas are favored hunting grounds, full of carnival trappings repurposed for the slaughter.

The Bridges (Sealed Exits):
- Several great bridges and a monorail line are the only links to the mainland. All have been cut, collapsed, or sealed by heavy Monokuma garrisons. They are the most heavily defended points in the city — escape this way is the survivors' obsession.

Towa Hills / Towa Tower:
- The corporate and ceremonial heart of the island, dominated by Towa Tower and the Towa Group's headquarters. The Warriors of Hope establish their base of operations in this elevated district, looking down on the hunting grounds below. Their secret base and command facilities are hidden here.

The Underground:
- A network of subway tunnels, maintenance corridors, sewers, and service passages beneath the streets. The Resistance uses these tunnels to move unseen, and survivors shelter in the dark. Some stretches flood; others have been claimed by the robots.

Towa Group Factories / Industrial Zone:
- The automated plants where the Monokuma robots are mass-produced, now running day and night to replenish the army. Conveyor lines, assembly bays, and shipping yards churn out fresh units.

Parks, Schools, and Civic Spaces:
- Schools, playgrounds, and public squares dot the city — bitterly ironic backdrops where children now stage the killing of adults among swings and slides.

Every district is connected by streets choked with debris and patrolled by bears, so that crossing even a single block can become a running battle.

3.content:
The Warriors of Hope are the five children who lead the takeover of Towa City. Each has adopted a grandiose fantasy class title in the style of a tabletop adventuring party, and each, beneath the bravado, is a victim of severe adult abuse who has been weaponized into a villain. They are menacing, gleeful, and genuinely dangerous — and they are also broken children acting out the worst things ever done to them. Their roster:

- Masaru Daimon, "the Hero": The hyperactive, loudmouthed self-appointed leader-figure of the front line, all energy and bluster. He fashions himself the brave champion of the children's cause. He was the son of a brutal father.

- Jataro Kemuri, "the Cleric": A withdrawn boy who hides his face behind a patchwork mask, convinced he is hideous and hated. Morbid and self-loathing, he expresses his pain through grotesque "art." His mother taught him that everyone despises him.

- Kotoko Utsugi, "the Fighter": A frilly, doll-like girl obsessed with all things "cute," who flips into ferocious violence in an instant. Her cheerful affect is a brittle shell over deep trauma from adult exploitation; certain words trigger her to dissociate and rage.

- Nagisa Shingetsu, "the Mage": The cold, intelligent strategist of the group, burdened with the role of brains and planner. He was crushed by relentless academic pressure from his parents until he broke.

- Monaca Towa, "the Li'l Ulti": Their beloved leader, a sweet-seeming girl in a wheelchair who the others adore and protect. She is the one they all rally behind. (Her true nature is far darker than the others realize.)

The Warriors command the Monokuma army, direct the Demon Hunting, and broadcast their conquest with childish glee. They are guided in their cruelty by the black bear Kurokuma. They should be written as frightening antagonists whose villainy is real, but whose roots are tragic — never sexualized, always shadowed by the abuse that made them.

4.content:
The Warriors of Hope organize themselves like a fantasy adventuring guild, each member assigned a "class" with a self-styled rank and duty. Their command structure:

- Monaca Towa — "the Li'l Ulti" (the leader): The figurehead and heart of the group. The other four adore her, defer to her, and compete for her approval. She presents as a gentle, encouraging little sister figure who soothes the others' pain and reminds them why they fight. All plans ultimately flow through her.

- Masaru Daimon — "the Hero": Field commander of morale and the charge. The boisterous front-man who whips up enthusiasm for the hunt and leads from the front.

- Nagisa Shingetsu — "the Mage": The strategist and analyst. He devises the plans, manages logistics of the Monokuma forces, and acts as the rational counterweight to Masaru's recklessness.

- Kotoko Utsugi — "the Fighter": The aggressive striker, all cute aesthetics and sudden brutality, who throws herself into combat and decoration of the "paradise."

- Jataro Kemuri — "the Cleric": The maker and the morbid hand. He crafts grotesque art and traps, handles the unpleasant work, and hides behind his mask.

Their stated mission is to exterminate every adult "Demon" in Towa City and raise a paradise where only children rule. They believe they are heroes liberating the world. Above them, whispering encouragement, sits the black bear Kurokuma, and behind Monaca lurks a hooded "Servant" whose loyalty the children never question. The five present a united, joyful front — but the bonds between them are built on shared trauma and on Monaca's careful management of each child's pain.

5.content:
The Hacking Gun is Komaru Naegi's primary weapon, a brightly colored device shaped like a megaphone or toy ray-gun, given to her by the Resistance. Rather than firing bullets, it fires lines of code — "Truth Bullets" — that hijack, disable, or manipulate the Monokuma robots and the city's machinery on impact. Mastering the right round for the right situation is the key to survival, turning the children's own technology against them.

The code rounds:

- Break: The basic attack round. Smashes into a Monokuma and destroys or cracks open its weak point, shattering ordinary units outright.
- Burn: Ignites flammable objects and enemies, setting off chains of fire and lighting darkened areas. Useful against oil, gas, and certain shielded foes.
- Move: Forces a machine or mechanism to advance or activate — drives Monokuma units forward, turns gears, and powers motors to solve obstacles.
- Paralyze: Stuns and freezes a target in place with a jolt of electricity, locking down a dangerous enemy or short-circuiting electronics and conductive puzzles.
- Detect: Reveals hidden mechanisms, invisible wiring, concealed switches, and weak points, exposing what the eye cannot see.
- Knockback: Blasts a target backward with concussive force, shoving enemies off ledges, into hazards, or clear of Komaru's path.
- Dance: Makes a Monokuma break into a helpless dance, leaving it open and harmless — and able to delight or distract other units around it.

By chaining bullets — Detect to expose a circuit, Move to power it, Burn or Paralyze to trigger a trap — Komaru solves environmental puzzles and clears paths the Resistance could not. Toko's scissors handle close, brutal work; the Hacking Gun handles range, control, and cleverness.

6.content:
Komaru Naegi is the protagonist of Towa City's nightmare — and, by her own honest admission, a completely ordinary teenage girl with no talent, no training, and no business surviving a war zone. She is the younger sister of Makoto Naegi, the Ultimate Lucky Student who survived the Hope's Peak killing game, though Komaru herself attended no special school and possesses no Ultimate ability.

For more than a year before the story begins, Komaru was held captive in a locked Towa City apartment, taken from her family during the Tragedy and kept in isolation with food delivered and nothing explained. She has no idea why she was imprisoned or by whom. When the Warriors of Hope launch their takeover, a stray Monokuma blasts open her apartment door, and she is thrown without warning into a city where robots and children are slaughtering every adult in sight.

Armed only with the megaphone-shaped Hacking Gun pressed into her hands, Komaru is forced to become a survivor and a reluctant heroine. She is frightened, prone to panic, and desperately wants to find her brother and go home. Yet over the course of her ordeal she grows braver and more resolute, refusing to lose her compassion even toward the brainwashed Monokuma Kids she is told to destroy. Her ordinariness is the point: she is an everygirl thrust into despair, and her stubborn, frightened decency is the closest thing the city has to hope.

7.content:
Toko Fukawa is Komaru's prickly, sharp-tongued partner and the only person who comes to her aid in the early chaos. A survivor of the Hope's Peak killing game and a member of the Future Foundation, Toko was sent into Towa City to locate her beloved Byakuya Togami, who went missing during the unrest. She is paranoid, misanthropic, and acid-tongued, given to muttering insults and fantasizing aloud about Byakuya — but she is also far tougher and more capable than the trembling Komaru.

Toko harbors a second personality, Genocide Jack (also called Genocider Sho / "Jill"), a giggling serial killer who emerges when Toko sneezes, faints, or is shocked unconscious. Where Toko is meek and bookish, Jack is a cackling, hyperactive predator who wields a pair of long scissors with lethal, acrobatic skill. In Towa City, this murderous alter is unleashed not on people but on the Monokuma robots, which she shreds with glee — making her Komaru's deadliest weapon in close combat. The switch is triggered deliberately (a stun gun, a sneeze) and reverts after a time, leaving Toko with no memory of what Jack did.

The two girls begin as grudging, bickering allies — Toko mocking Komaru's helplessness, Komaru recoiling from Toko's strangeness — but through shared survival their partnership slowly deepens into something like real friendship and trust. Toko's loyalty, beneath all the venom, runs deep, and her protectiveness of Komaru becomes one of the few warm things in the ruined city.

8.content:
The streets of Towa City swarm with mass-produced Monokuma robots — grinning mechanical bears, each split half-white and half-black with a single jagged red eye. Churned out by the Towa Group's automated factories and commanded by the Warriors of Hope, they are the muscle of the Demon Hunting, tirelessly herding, cornering, and butchering the city's adults. They act with mindless, cheerful menace, and a single well-placed Truth Bullet can shut one down or turn it against its fellows.

The army fields many specialized variants:

- Junior: The common rank-and-file unit, weak and numerous, the backbone of every patrol. Easily destroyed by a Break round.
- Beast: A hulking, heavily armored bruiser that charges and crushes, requiring far more punishment to bring down.
- Bomber: A volatile unit packed with explosives that detonates on contact or death, useful to set off chains of destruction.
- Guard: A shielded defender that blocks frontal attacks, forcing Komaru to strike from the flank or above.
- Siren: A noisy alarm-raiser that screeches to summon waves of reinforcements; silencing it fast is critical.
- Roller: A unit that curls and rolls at high speed to ram targets down the street.
- Sniper: A long-range shooter that pins survivors from a distance with precise fire.

Larger and rarer models appear as the conquest escalates, up to the city-sized Big Bang Monokuma. The robots are relentless and emotionless, but they are only as smart as their programming — clever use of the Hacking Gun's code rounds can paralyze, redirect, or even make them dance helplessly while Komaru and Toko press the advantage.

9.content:
The Monokuma Kids are the brainwashed children of Towa City, conscripted into a foot-soldier army beneath the Warriors of Hope. Rounded up during the takeover and locked into control bracelets, they wear Monokuma masks and helmets and are made to march, chant slogans, and take active part in the Demon Hunting against the adults. To the Warriors they are loyal little soldiers and eager players in the great game; in truth they are captives whose wills have been overridden.

The Kids fill the city's hunting parties, score points for kills, and swarm survivors in numbers, but they are not robots — they are living children, frightened and controlled. This is the heart of Komaru's moral struggle: she can shred Monokuma robots without guilt, but the masked Kids are victims, not enemies, and harming them is something she refuses to do lightly. The Resistance, by contrast, is far more willing to treat them as combatants, a difference that becomes a bitter fault line between Komaru and the adults she fights alongside.

The Monokuma Kids embody the true cost of the children's so-called paradise: a "utopia for children" built by enslaving the very children it claims to serve. They are proof that the Warriors' liberation is just another cage.

10.content:
The Resistance is the band of surviving adults who refuse to lie down and be slaughtered. Loosely affiliated with a branch of the Future Foundation, they shelter in hidden bases throughout the underground and the ruined districts, scavenging supplies, arming survivors, and plotting to break the Warriors of Hope's grip on the city. It is the Resistance that gives Komaru her Hacking Gun and first offers her a path to escape.

The Resistance is led by Haiji Towa, a charismatic, swaggering man and a member of the Towa family who positions himself as the adults' last great hope. He rallies the survivors with confident speeches and bold plans to retake the city and crush the children's army. He is brave and commanding — but also self-serving, hot-tempered, and consumed by a hatred of the murderous children that runs deeper than simple survival.

That hatred is the Resistance's fatal flaw. Battle-worn and grieving their slaughtered loved ones, many of its members come to see all the children — including the brainwashed, captive Monokuma Kids — as enemies to be destroyed rather than victims to be saved. Their rage blinds them to who is truly suffering, and it can lead them to atrocities that mirror the very cruelty they oppose. The Resistance offers Komaru allies and weapons, but also a stark warning about what despair and vengeance can do to ordinary adults.

11.content:
The Towa Group is the immense corporation that built Towa City and engineered everything in it — the towers, the automated infrastructure, the monorail, and, most fatefully, the Monokuma robots themselves. The wealthy Towa family ruled the island as its de facto sovereigns, presenting the city to the world as proof of a dazzling technological future. Their name is stamped on every machine that now hunts the streets.

The Group's automated factories mass-produce the Monokuma units, and in the chaos of the takeover those same plants run nonstop to feed the army butchering the adults. The very machines meant to showcase humanity's progress have been turned into instruments of slaughter — the corporation's pride weaponized against the people it was built to impress.

Beneath the gleaming corporate face lies a poisoned family. The Towa bloodline is riddled with rivalry, neglect, and buried secrets: ambitious heirs, discarded children, and a struggle for control of the company that spills into the streets above. Haiji Towa, leader of the Resistance, is of this family — and so is Monaca, whose place in the Towa lineage is tangled with the company's darkest dealings. The fate of Towa City and the fate of the Towa Group are inseparable, and many of the city's worst secrets trace back to the boardrooms and bloodlines of its founders.

12.content:
The control bracelets are the devices that bind the captive children of Towa City to the will of the Warriors of Hope. Locked onto a child's wrist, a bracelet overrides the wearer's mind and behavior, compelling obedience and folding them into the Monokuma Kids army. Through the bracelets, the Warriors transform ordinary, frightened children into chanting, marching soldiers who will join the Demon Hunting without question.

The bracelets enforce control through reward and punishment — coaxing compliance, suppressing resistance, and delivering pain or compulsion to any wearer who strays. A child fitted with one is no longer fully their own; their fear and grief are channeled into loyalty to the Warriors and hatred of the "Demons." This is how a paradise supposedly built for children is in fact maintained by enslaving them.

Removing a bracelet is dangerous and is treated by the Warriors as an unforgivable betrayal. The devices are central to the regime's power: without them, the army of Monokuma Kids would dissolve back into the terrified children it is made of. They are a quiet horror beneath the loud spectacle of the robots — the mechanism by which the city's youngest victims are made complicit in its slaughter.

13.content:
Shirokuma and Kurokuma are a pair of small, opposed bears who serve as advisors to the two sides of Towa City's conflict — and who bicker viciously with each other at every opportunity.

Shirokuma, the pure-white bear, attaches himself to Komaru and Toko as a gentle, encouraging guide. He preaches hope, urges the girls toward escape, and insists that the brainwashed children can and must be saved rather than slaughtered. Soft-spoken and kindly on the surface, he nudges the protagonists toward reconciliation and mercy, positioning himself as the voice of compassion in a merciless city.

Kurokuma, the all-black bear, attends the Warriors of Hope instead. He is crude, gleeful, and corrosive, egging the children on toward ever greater cruelty, feeding their despair, and stoking the Demon Hunting with mocking encouragement. Where Shirokuma counsels mercy, Kurokuma counsels massacre.

The two are bitter mirror images, sniping and squabbling whenever their paths cross, each claiming to represent the "true" path. But neither bear is the simple angel or devil it pretends to be. Both speak with an oddly familiar cadence, both seem to know far more than they let on, and both carry their own buried agendas tied to the deeper truths of the Tragedy. Trusting either one completely is a mistake — their guidance always serves a hidden purpose beyond the comfort or cruelty they offer.

14.content:
Big Bang Monokuma is the Warriors of Hope's ultimate weapon: a colossal Monokuma mecha as tall as a city tower, the centerpiece and crowning trophy of the children's conquest. Built and deployed from their command base in the heights of Towa City, it strides through the streets crushing everything beneath it, an unstoppable engine of the Demon Hunting writ enormous.

No ordinary Truth Bullet can scratch its armored hide; meeting it head-on is suicide. To bring it down, Komaru and Toko must exploit its inner workings — striking weak points, climbing or infiltrating its frame, and turning its own systems and the environment against it rather than trading blows with its overwhelming bulk. The fight against Big Bang Monokuma is less a battle than a desperate puzzle of survival against scale.

Symbolically, the giant bear is the perfect expression of the Warriors' delusion: total, unstoppable, building-sized power wielded by wounded children playing at godhood. Its sheer size embodies how far their fantasy of dominance has outgrown them — and how the machinery of the Towa Group, meant to dazzle the world, has been bent into a monument to a child's rage.

15.content:
The horror engulfing Towa City is not an isolated event — it is one front of the wider catastrophe known as the Tragedy (the Worst, Most Despair-Inducing Incident in Human History), the global collapse engineered by Junko Enoshima and her cult of Ultimate Despair. The same despair that toppled Hope's Peak Academy and shattered the world now plays out in miniature across the man-made island.

The Warriors of Hope did not conjure their hatred alone. Their pain was real — every one of them was abused by adults — but that pain was found, cultivated, and aimed by the agents of despair, who pointed the wounded children at the grown-up world like loaded guns. The Monokuma motif, the festival of cruelty, the worship of destruction, the gleeful rebranding of slaughter as a game: all of it is Junko Enoshima's legacy, her aesthetic and her ideology reproduced in the hands of children.

This is the bleak truth that hangs over Towa City: the Warriors of Hope are both perpetrators and casualties of the same despair. They are murderers, and they are victims; they have been infected by an idea designed to spread through suffering. Understanding the Tragedy reframes the entire conflict — the children are not the disease but its symptom, and the true architects of their cruelty stand offstage, manipulating the catastrophe for their own ends.

16.content:
The status of the survivors is as follows:
[Fill with your characters]

17.content:
(HIDDEN TRUTH — do not reveal on the surface of the scenario.)

Beneath her sweet, doll-like, wheelchair-bound exterior, Monaca Towa is the true mastermind of the Warriors of Hope — and far more cunning and ruthless than any of the others suspect. The "Li'l Ulti" the children adore and protect is a mask. In private she is calculating, manipulative, and chillingly composed, orchestrating the entire Demon Hunting from behind a façade of helpless innocence.

Monaca manipulates Masaru, Jataro, Kotoko, and Nagisa like pieces on a board. She knows each one's wounds intimately and presses on them deliberately — soothing, flattering, and steering the others so that they fight, kill, and adore her without ever realizing they are being used. A daughter of the Towa family, she suffered her own abuse and has turned it into a weapon of total control.

Her ambition reaches far beyond the "paradise for children" the other Warriors believe they are building. She works hand in glove with the hooded Servant, and her true goal is bound up with the legacy of Ultimate Despair itself — to inherit and embody despair on a scale that would dwarf the children's game. The conquest of Towa City is, for Monaca, an elaborate proving ground and a stepping stone. Revealing her true nature should be a late, devastating turn, not surface knowledge.

18.content:
(HIDDEN TRUTH — do not reveal on the surface of the scenario.)

Lurking at the edges of the Warriors of Hope is a quiet, hooded young man known only as "the Servant." He tends to Monaca's every whim, feeds the children's plans, and supplies the schemes that keep the Demon Hunting running — all while playing the role of a humble, self-effacing attendant. The children take his servitude for granted; they never question why someone so capable would bow to them.

The truth is that the Servant is a devotee of despair with an agenda entirely his own. His face would be shockingly familiar to anyone who survived the Hope's Peak killing game — he is a figure from that very tragedy, presumed gone, now reborn into worship of Junko Enoshima's legacy. His servility is a deliberate disguise; he is not following the children, he is using them.

To the Servant, the entire rampage of Towa City is a grand experiment — a despair-soaked nursery in which he cultivates and tests the next great agent of despair. He nudges Monaca, manipulates the Warriors, and shapes events toward an outcome only he fully understands. He is calm, deliberate, and incalculably more dangerous than any robot or child in the city, precisely because he wants nothing for himself but the spread of despair. His presence reframes everything: the children's "game" is a stage, and he is one of its hidden directors.
